settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`src/extension.ts` is the extension entrypoint and wires activation, commands, and views. `src/components/` contains UI-facing providers and panels such as the status bar item, checkpoint tree, and active session tree. `src/checkpoints/` holds the session/checkpoint domain logic, git enrichment, storage, transcript parsing, and search helpers. General utilities live in top-level `src/*.ts` files such as `workspaceProbe.ts` and `runCommand.ts`. Tests live under `src/test/`, with reusable checkpoint fixtures in `src/test/checkpoints/fixtures/`. `media/` stores extension icons. Treat `dist/` and `out/` as generated output.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm compile`: run type-checking, ESLint, and the esbuild bundle into `dist/extension.js`.
- `pnpm watch`: run esbuild watch mode and `tsc --watch` together for local development.
- `pnpm check-types`: run strict TypeScript checks only.
- `pnpm lint`: lint `src/` with the repository ESLint config.
- `pnpm test`: compile tests, rebuild the extension, and run the VS Code test harness.
- `pnpm package`: build the production bundle used for publishing.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Use strict TypeScript and follow the existing code style: tabs for indentation, semicolons, and mostly single-quoted imports/strings. Use `PascalCase` for classes, providers, and exported types; use `camelCase` for functions and locals; use `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for extension-wide constants. Keep command and context identifiers namespaced, for example `session.bridge.entire.refresh`. Let ESLint catch small consistency issues, and do not hand-edit generated files in `dist/` or `out/`.

## Testing Guidelines
Write tests as `*.test.ts` under `src/test/`, mirroring the feature area when possible, such as `src/test/checkpoints/`. This project uses Mocha with `@vscode/test-cli` and `vscode-test`, compiling test files to `out/test/**/*.test.js`. Prefer fixture-backed tests for checkpoint parsing, git metadata joins, and tree/detail view models. No formal coverage threshold is configured, but every behavior change should include or update targeted tests.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Recent history uses short, imperative commit subjects, sometimes with lightweight prefixes such as `feat:` or `refactor:`. Keep each commit focused on one change and describe behavior, not process. Pull requests should summarize the user-visible effect, list verification commands run, link the relevant issue when applicable, and include screenshots or recordings for tree view, panel, or status bar changes.

## Configuration Notes
This extension targets VS Code `^1.110.0` and assumes an Entire-enabled Git repository at runtime. For tests and local development, prefer checked-in fixtures and mocks over machine-specific `.entire` state or hard-coded paths.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `dela/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: Update `listSessions` in [orchestration.ts](src/checkpoints/orchestration.ts) to use sessionPaths to fetch sessions if it's not empty and revert to using the checkpoint id if empty. Wire this into [sessionsTreeView.ts](src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts) [extension.ts](src/extension.ts) [checkpointTreeView.ts](src/components/checkpointTreeView.ts) to make sure whenever a section is selected the necessary model is populated and the sessions for the selected checkpoint is loaded. We want to avoid reloading and processing data that has been done already when checkpoints are loaded when we want to load sessions for the selected checkpoint. Update the documentations and necessary tests after the change.

Updated the checkpoint-session loading path so it reuses the `sessionPaths` that were already resolved by the checkpoint tree.

The main change is in [orchestration.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/orchestration.ts): `listSessions` now takes `(repoPath, checkpointId, sessionPaths)`, loads sessions directly from `sessionPaths` when present, and only falls back to `getCheckpointSummary(checkpointId)` when that array is empty. I pushed the same path-aware contract down through the checkpoint stores in [types.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/types.ts), [store.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/store.ts), [gitStore.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/gitStore.ts), [fsStore.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/fsStore.ts), and [checkpointRemote.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/checkpointRemote.ts) so the fast path is real and doesn’t re-read the selected checkpoint summary.

I rewired the selection flow in [checkpointTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/checkpointTreeView.ts), [sessionsTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts), and [extension.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/extension.ts) so a checkpoint selection now carries `checkpointId + sessionPaths + commitSha`, the sessions provider starts loading immediately on selection, and reloads skip invalid empty-selection loads. I also updated [README.md](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/README.md), [CHANGELOG.md](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/CHANGELOG.md), and the related tests in [module.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/checkpoints/module.test.ts), [sessionsUi.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/components/sessionsUi.test.ts), [checkpointUi.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/components/checkpointUi.test.ts), and [workspaceProbe.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/workspaceProbe.test.ts). While running the full suite I also fixed an existing Cursor transcript rendering issue in [nativeTranscript.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/nativeTranscript.ts) and raised two slow integration-suite timeouts so the VS Code test host run stays green.

Verified with `pnpm test`.

`CheckpointSessionSelection` in [sessionsTreeView.ts](src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts) and `CheckpointSelectionContext` in [checkpointTreeView.ts](src/components/checkpointTreeView.ts) perform same function. Get rid of one

Removed the duplicate checkpoint-selection type and kept `CheckpointSessionSelection` in [sessionsTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts) as the single contract. [checkpointTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/checkpointTreeView.ts) now imports that type and uses it for `selectionContext` and `getCheckpointSelectionContext()`.

Verified with `pnpm check-types` and `pnpm test -- --grep "Sessions UI|Checkpoint UI"`; both passed.

[sessionsTreeView.ts](src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts) should not use activeSessions from [workspaceProbe.ts](src/workspaceProbe.ts) . Should be keeping it's own state of sessions for selected checkpoint. When clicked,  the  details in [sessionDetailsPanel.ts](src/components/sessionDetailsPanel.ts) should always load the session details for the selected section with no fallback to any other session

Changed the sessions tree so it no longer reads `workspaceProbe.activeSessions` for its data. In [sessionsTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts), the provider now owns its loaded cards in both modes: checkpoint sessions stay selection-driven, and the active view reloads itself through `listActiveSessions` instead of seeding from the probe snapshot. I also updated [extension.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/extension.ts) so probe refreshes trigger an active-session reload instead of a passive repaint.

For the details flow, [orchestration.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/checkpoints/orchestration.ts) now treats checkpoint-backed detail requests as strict: if the selected tree item does not carry `checkpointEntries`, `getSessionDetail()` returns `null` rather than falling back through live-session state or any other checkpoint/session. That keeps [sessionDetailsPanel.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/sessionDetailsPanel.ts) bound to the exact selected checkpoint session.

Updated tests in [sessionsUi.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/components/sessionsUi.test.ts) and [module.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/checkpoints/module.test.ts). Verified with `pnpm test`.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `dela/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

<apps_instructions>
## Apps (Connectors)
Apps (Connectors) can be explicitly triggered in user messages in the format `[$app-name](app://{connector_id})`. Apps can also be implicitly triggered as long as the context suggests usage of available apps.
An app is equivalent to a set of MCP tools within the `codex_apps` MCP.
An installed app's MCP tools are either provided to you already, or can be lazy-loaded through the `tool_search` tool. If `tool_search` is available, the apps that are searchable by `tools_search` will be listed by it.
Do not additionally call list_mcp_resources or list_mcp_resource_templates for apps.
</apps_instructions>

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`src/extension.ts` is the extension entrypoint and wires activation, commands, and views. `src/components/` contains UI-facing providers and panels such as the status bar item, checkpoint tree, and active session tree. `src/checkpoints/` holds the session/checkpoint domain logic, git enrichment, storage, transcript parsing, and search helpers. General utilities live in top-level `src/*.ts` files such as `workspaceProbe.ts` and `runCommand.ts`. Tests live under `src/test/`, with reusable checkpoint fixtures in `src/test/checkpoints/fixtures/`. `media/` stores extension icons. Treat `dist/` and `out/` as generated output.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm compile`: run type-checking, ESLint, and the esbuild bundle into `dist/extension.js`.
- `pnpm watch`: run esbuild watch mode and `tsc --watch` together for local development.
- `pnpm check-types`: run strict TypeScript checks only.
- `pnpm lint`: lint `src/` with the repository ESLint config.
- `pnpm test`: compile tests, rebuild the extension, and run the VS Code test harness.
- `pnpm package`: build the production bundle used for publishing.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Use strict TypeScript and follow the existing code style: tabs for indentation, semicolons, and mostly single-quoted imports/strings. Use `PascalCase` for classes, providers, and exported types; use `camelCase` for functions and locals; use `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for extension-wide constants. Keep command and context identifiers namespaced, for example `session.bridge.entire.refresh`. Let ESLint catch small consistency issues, and do not hand-edit generated files in `dist/` or `out/`.

## Testing Guidelines
Write tests as `*.test.ts` under `src/test/`, mirroring the feature area when possible, such as `src/test/checkpoints/`. This project uses Mocha with `@vscode/test-cli` and `vscode-test`, compiling test files to `out/test/**/*.test.js`. Prefer fixture-backed tests for checkpoint parsing, git metadata joins, and tree/detail view models. No formal coverage threshold is configured, but every behavior change should include or update targeted tests.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Recent history uses short, imperative commit subjects, sometimes with lightweight prefixes such as `feat:` or `refactor:`. Keep each commit focused on one change and describe behavior, not process. Pull requests should summarize the user-visible effect, list verification commands run, link the relevant issue when applicable, and include screenshots or recordings for tree view, panel, or status bar changes.

## Configuration Notes
This extension targets VS Code `^1.110.0` and assumes an Entire-enabled Git repository at runtime. For tests and local development, prefer checked-in fixtures and mocks over machine-specific `.entire` state or hard-coded paths.

</INSTRUCTIONS>

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `dela/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.aggregated_output:
# Session Bridge

Session Bridge is a VS Code extension for browsing Entire checkpoints, committed checkpoint sessions, and live Entire session state from the current repository.

## Features

- Browse committed checkpoints grouped by authored date.
- Inspect live Entire sessions for the current repository.
- Select a checkpoint to load only the sessions captured in that checkpoint.
- Reuse checkpoint summary `sessionPaths` that were already resolved by the Checkpoints tree, so selecting a checkpoint does not reprocess the same checkpoint summary just to locate its session files again.

For example if there is an image subfolder under your extension project workspace:

\!\[feature X\]\(images/feature-x.png\)

> Tip: Many popular extensions utilize animations. This is an excellent way to show off your extension! We recommend short, focused animations that are easy to follow.

## Requirements

If you have any requirements or dependencies, add a section describing those and how to install and configure them.

## Extension Settings

Include if your extension adds any VS Code settings through the `contributes.configuration` extension point.

For example:

This extension contributes the following settings:

* `myExtension.enable`: Enable/disable this extension.
* `myExtension.thing`: Set to `blah` to do something.

## Known Issues

Calling out known issues can help limit users opening duplicate issues against your extension.

## Release Notes

Users appreciate release notes as you update your extension.

### 1.0.0

Initial release of ...

### 1.0.1

Fixed issue #.

### 1.1.0

Added features X, Y, and Z.

---

## Following extension guidelines

Ensure that you've read through the extensions guidelines and follow the best practices for creating your extension.

* [Extension Guidelines](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/extension-guidelines)

## Working with Markdown

You can author your README using Visual Studio Code. Here are some useful editor keyboard shortcuts:

* Split the editor (`Cmd+\` on macOS or `Ctrl+\` on Windows and Linux).
* Toggle preview (`Shift+Cmd+V` on macOS or `Shift+Ctrl+V` on Windows and Linux).
* Press `Ctrl+Space` (Windows, Linux, macOS) to see a list of Markdown snippets.

## For more information

* [Visual Studio Code's Markdown Support](http://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/markdown)
* [Markdown Syntax Reference](https://help.github.com/articles/markdown-basics/)

**Enjoy!**

# Change Log

All notable changes to the "session-bridge" extension will be documented in this file.

Check [Keep a Changelog](http://keepachangelog.com/) for recommendations on how to structure this file.

payload.message:
Removed the active-session surface from the Sessions tree and its related extension wiring. [sessionsTreeView.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/components/sessionsTreeView.ts) is now checkpoint-only: it no longer carries active/live session branches, no longer exposes doctor actions, and its transcript action always targets committed checkpoint data. I also removed the old active-session command residue from [extension.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/extension.ts) and [package.json](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/package.json), so the extension no longer contributes the active sessions view, `runDoctor`, or `resumeBranch` through this surface.

I updated the UI and manifest coverage in [sessionsUi.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/components/sessionsUi.test.ts) and [extension.test.ts](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/src/test/extension.test.ts), and refreshed the wording in [README.md](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/README.md) and [CHANGELOG.md](/Users/savekirk/dev/ai/entire/session-bridge/CHANGELOG.md) so they describe a checkpoint-driven Sessions tree instead of a mixed live/checkpoint view.

Verified with `pnpm check-types`, `pnpm lint`, and `pnpm test`.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
`src/extension.ts` is the extension entrypoint and wires activation, commands, and views. `src/components/` contains UI-facing providers and panels such as the status bar item, checkpoint tree, and active session tree. `src/checkpoints/` holds the session/checkpoint domain logic, git enrichment, storage, transcript parsing, and search helpers. General utilities live in top-level `src/*.ts` files such as `workspaceProbe.ts` and `runCommand.ts`. Tests live under `src/test/`, with reusable checkpoint fixtures in `src/test/checkpoints/fixtures/`. `media/` stores extension icons. Treat `dist/` and `out/` as generated output.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm compile`: run type-checking, ESLint, and the esbuild bundle into `dist/extension.js`.
- `pnpm watch`: run esbuild watch mode and `tsc --watch` together for local development.
- `pnpm check-types`: run strict TypeScript checks only.
- `pnpm lint`: lint `src/` with the repository ESLint config.
- `pnpm test`: compile tests, rebuild the extension, and run the VS Code test harness.
- `pnpm package`: build the production bundle used for publishing.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
Use strict TypeScript and follow the existing code style: tabs for indentation, semicolons, and mostly single-quoted imports/strings. Use `PascalCase` for classes, providers, and exported types; use `camelCase` for functions and locals; use `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for extension-wide constants. Keep command and context identifiers namespaced, for example `session.bridge.entire.refresh`. Let ESLint catch small consistency issues, and do not hand-edit generated files in `dist/` or `out/`.

## Testing Guidelines
Write tests as `*.test.ts` under `src/test/`, mirroring the feature area when possible, such as `src/test/checkpoints/`. This project uses Mocha with `@vscode/test-cli` and `vscode-test`, compiling test files to `out/test/**/*.test.js`. Prefer fixture-backed tests for checkpoint parsing, git metadata joins, and tree/detail view models. No formal coverage threshold is configured, but every behavior change should include or update targeted tests.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
Recent history uses short, imperative commit subjects, sometimes with lightweight prefixes such as `feat:` or `refactor:`. Keep each commit focused on one change and describe behavior, not process. Pull requests should summarize the user-visible effect, list verification commands run, link the relevant issue when applicable, and include screenshots or recordings for tree view, panel, or status bar changes.

## Configuration Notes
This extension targets VS Code `^1.110.0` and assumes an Entire-enabled Git repository at runtime. For tests and local development, prefer checked-in fixtures and mocks over machine-specific `.entire` state or hard-coded paths.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `dela/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
